title: Blood on Leather
timeEstimate: Pro-lane; home is blot + condition only
stainChemistry: Blood is blood pigment protein + iron (oxidizes Fe²⁺ → Fe³⁺ over hours). Leather is animal hide, finished or aniline. Two paths: (1) FINISHED leather has a polymer topcoat — fresh blood often blots off the surface before reaching the dyed hide; gentle soap + condition handles light cases. (2) ANILINE leather absorbs blood directly into the dyed-through hide — pro-only, no DIY. Critical leather rule: NO oxidizers (peroxide / OxiClean / chlorine) — strip finish + dye + oils permanently. Pro uses sodium hydrosulfite (REDUCING bleach, leather-safe) for oxidized blood pigment. Standard blood-removal toolkit (peroxide on cotton) destroys leather.
whyThisWorks: Quick blot prevents blood from soaking past the topcoat. Mild soap + minimum water + immediate conditioning preserves the hide. Sodium hydrosulfite is the only safe oxidized-blood pigment chemistry on leather, and it's pro-only. Home protocol: blot, mild soap if finished + visible bulk, condition, escalate.
defaultAssumption: Finished leather assumed; aniline = pro from minute one. Most car interior / handbag / shoe leather is finished. High-end aniline upholstery exists. Always condition after any wet treatment.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Gloves on (biohazard). Water-drop test on hidden leather: beads = finished, soaks in = aniline (pro from minute one). Aniline absorbs blood directly into the dyed hide.
Dampen white cloth with COLD water (cold prevents protein from setting). Press straight down on blood. Lift, fresh part of cloth, repeat. No rubbing — pushes blood past the topcoat.

spottingProtocol.equipment: 1–2 drops Dawn in cup of cold water, white cloth wrung nearly dry
spottingProtocol.instruction: 1–2 drops Dawn in cold water. Wring cloth nearly dry. Wipe outside-in. Plain-water-damp wipe to clear soap. Leather should never look visibly wet.

spottingProtocol.technique: Reducing bleach for oxidized blood pigment
spottingProtocol.instruction: Pro-only step. Reducing bleach reverses Fe³⁺ → Fe²⁺ on leather without stripping finish. Home cannot replicate safely. NEVER use peroxide as substitute — strips leather.
Mandatory after any wet treatment. Cleaning strips natural oils — un-conditioned leather cracks.

steps: 1. Water-drop test for leather type. Aniline = pro from minute one.
2. Gloves. Cold damp-cloth press-blot. Cold prevents protein setting.
3. Mild soap on barely-damp cloth, outside-in.
4. Plain-water damp wipe to clear soap.
5. Sodium hydrosulfite for oxidized blood pigment (REDUCING, not oxidizer).
professionalProtocol.temperature: cold for blood, ambient for conditioner — no heat (heat sets protein + dries leather)
warnings: NEVER hydrogen peroxide on leather — strips finish + dye + oils permanently
NEVER OxiClean / chlorine bleach — destroys leather
NEVER hot water — sets blood protein
NEVER acetone or undiluted alcohol — destroys leather finish
Aniline / unfinished leather = pro from minute one
Reducing bleach (sodium hydrosulfite) is the ONLY safe oxidized-blood pigment chemistry on leather — pro-only

homeSolutions.agent: Identify Your Leather First — One Drop of Water
homeSolutions.instruction: Drop ONE bead of water on a hidden leather area. If it BEADS on top = finished/pigmented (proceed cautiously). If it SOAKS IN immediately = aniline (STOP — pro from minute one). Aniline absorbs blood directly into the dyed hide; even fresh attempts risk staining the leather permanently.

homeSolutions.agent: What NOT To Do — The Bad-Advice List
homeSolutions.instruction: The internet's worst leather + blood advice: hydrogen peroxide, OxiClean, chlorine bleach, salt paste. ALL of them strip leather's finish + dye + natural oils, permanently. The damage is worse than the blood. Also: NO acetone, NO undiluted alcohol, NO baking soda paste. Your safe toolkit is exactly four things: gloves, mild soap (1–2 drops), water, leather conditioner. The peroxide-on-cotton blood-removal trick destroys leather.

Disposable gloves (biohazard, especially if not your blood). Dampen a clean white cloth with COLD water — cold prevents protein from setting. Press the cloth straight down on the blood. Lift straight up, press a fresh part of the cloth, repeat. Do NOT rub or wipe — rubbing pushes blood past wear spots in the topcoat into the dyed hide where home can't reach it.

homeSolutions.agent: Mild Soap on a BARELY-Damp Cloth
homeSolutions.instruction: Mix 1 to 2 drops of Dawn (just two drops) in a cup of COLD water. Dampen a clean white cloth, WRING IT OUT until barely damp — no dripping. Wipe the area in gentle circles, OUTSIDE-IN. Then wipe with a plain-water-damp cloth to clear soap. The leather should never look visibly wet.
Apply leather conditioner (Lexol or Bickmore at any drugstore / hardware store) with a soft cloth per the label. Cleaning strips leather's natural oils; conditioning prevents cracking over weeks.

homeSolutions.agent: Pro for Anything Remaining — What to Tell Them
homeSolutions.instruction: Take to a leather care specialist (NOT a regular dry cleaner). Tell them: "Blood on [finished / aniline / unknown] leather, age [hours / days], one home pass with mild soap + barely-damp cloth + conditioner. No oxidizer, no acetone, no alcohol, no hot water used." Pros use sodium hydrosulfite (REDUCING bleach, leather-safe) for oxidized blood pigment — chemistry home cannot do safely.

steps: 1. Water-drop test. Aniline = pro from minute one.
2. Gloves on. NO oxidizer, NO acetone, NO alcohol, NO baking soda.
4. Mild soap (1–2 drops Dawn) on a BARELY-damp cloth, outside-in.
5. Plain-water damp wipe to clear soap.
7. Residual = pro leather specialist. NO second home pass.
products: Mild dish soap (Dawn — 1–2 drops, no more)
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if leather is aniline / unfinished, if blood remains after one careful pass, or if anyone has applied harsh chemistry previously.
warnings: NEVER hydrogen peroxide on leather — strips finish permanently (the cotton trick)

neverDo: Never any oxidizer (peroxide / OxiClean / chlorine) on leather
Never hot water — sets blood protein
fiberSensitivities: Finished / pigmented leather: tolerates mild soap + barely-damp cloth + conditioner
Aniline / unfinished: pro-only — even water rings

materialWarnings: Peroxide is the worst piece of bad internet advice for blood on leather
Conditioner after every wet treatment, mandatory

escalation.when: Aniline / unfinished leather (always), blood remaining after one home pass, or anyone has applied oxidizer / acetone / alcohol previously.
escalation.whatToTell: "Blood on [finished / aniline / unknown] leather, age [hours / days], one home pass with mild soap + barely-damp cloth + conditioner. No oxidizer, no acetone, no alcohol, no hot water used [or: someone applied X before I got here]."
escalation.specialistType: leather care specialist (NOT a regular dry cleaner — confirm sodium hydrosulfite + leather-safe chemistry)

contextQuestions: Water-drop test: bead, soak, or in-between?
Furniture / handbag / jacket / shoes / car seat?
Color — light leather shows residual stain more
Has anyone applied peroxide / OxiClean / acetone / alcohol?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Aniline leather is the unforgiving variant. Car interior leather is usually heavily topcoated — more forgiving. Light-colored leather shows residual better. Pre-applied peroxide = leather likely already stripped where applied. Honest disclosure changes pro plan.
